eli3: Discrete distributions describe countable outcomes: coin flips, dice rolls, number of emails received. Each outcome has a probability mass function (PMF): P(X = k) for each possible k.

Common discrete distributions: Bernoulli (one yes/no trial), Binomial (count of successes in n trials), Poisson (count of rare events), Geometric (trials until first success). Each models a different counting scenario.
The sum of all probabilities must equal 1. The expected value is the weighted average of outcomes. Variance measures spread. These properties characterize the distribution's behavior.

whenWhy.title: Binary classification outcomes
whenWhy.body: Bernoulli models single binary outcomes. Logistic regression predicts P(Y=1|X). Binomial distribution for multiple independent binary trials (e.g., A/B test conversions).

whenWhy.title: Count data modeling
whenWhy.body: Poisson models rare event counts (emails per hour, website visits). Poisson regression (log link) for count outcomes. Negative Binomial generalizes Poisson with overdispersion.

whenWhy.title: Waiting times and reliability
whenWhy.body: Geometric: trials until first success. Used in reliability engineering, quality control. Memoryless property: P(X > m+n | X > m) = P(X > n) — past failures don't affect future.

whenWhy.title: A/B testing and conversions
whenWhy.body: Beta-Binomial conjugate pair for Bayesian A/B testing. Prior over conversion rate updated with Binomial likelihood. Multinomial for multiple categories (which of 3 landing pages converts).

deepDiveIntro: A discrete random variable takes values from a countable set — usually integers — and each value has an associated probability. The probabilities must all be non-negative and sum to exactly one. Different discrete distributions arise naturally from different counting scenarios: Bernoulli from a single yes/no trial, Binomial from counting successes in n trials, Poisson from counting rare events, Geometric from counting trials until the first success.

The expected value is the probability-weighted average of all possible outcomes; variance measures the average squared deviation from that average. The moment generating function encodes all moments of a distribution and is a useful tool for deriving properties and proving limit theorems.

deepDive.heading: Bernoulli and Binomial
deepDive.body: A Bernoulli trial is a single experiment with two outcomes — success or failure — with probability p of success. The Binomial distribution counts successes across n independent Bernoulli trials; its mean is n times p and variance is n times p times one minus p. As n grows large with the expected number of successes held constant, the Binomial approaches a Poisson distribution.

deepDive.heading: Poisson Distribution
deepDive.body: The Poisson distribution models the number of rare, independent events in a fixed interval — customer arrivals per minute, website requests per second, radioactive decays per hour. Its key property is that the mean and variance are both equal to the rate parameter. The sum of independent Poisson random variables is also Poisson, making it easy to combine counts from different sources.

deepDive.heading: Geometric and Negative Binomial
deepDive.body: The Geometric distribution models how many trials you need until you get the first success. It has the memoryless property — past failures provide no information about future success, so each trial is like starting fresh. The Negative Binomial generalises this to counting trials until r successes and is also used as an overdispersed alternative to the Poisson when the variance in count data exceeds the mean.

deepDive.heading: Multinomial Distribution
deepDive.body: The Multinomial distribution extends Binomial to more than two outcomes — think rolling a six-sided die n times and counting how many times each face appears. Each trial produces one of k categories; the result is a vector of counts that must sum to n. This distribution is the foundation for topic models in NLP and Bayesian categorical modelling.

deepDive.heading: Hypergeometric Distribution
deepDive.body: The Hypergeometric distribution describes sampling without replacement. You have a population of N items, K of which are 'successes'; if you draw n without replacing, the count of successes follows the Hypergeometric distribution. It differs from the Binomial (which assumes replacement, so each draw is independent). Used in quality control audits, survey sampling, and card probability problems.

takeaways: Bernoulli: single binary trial. Binomial: sum of n Bernoullis.
Poisson: rare events, E[X]=Var(X)=λ. Limit of Binomial.
Geometric: waiting time until first success. Memoryless.

edgeCases.q: What if Poisson variance exceeds mean?
edgeCases.a: Poisson requires mean = variance. Overdispersion (Var > Mean) suggests unmodeled heterogeneity. Use Negative Binomial instead (has extra dispersion parameter). Underdispersion (Var < Mean) is rarer but can be modeled with Conway-Maxwell-Poisson or binomial.
When n ≥ 20 and p ≤ 0.05 (or np ≤ 10), Binomial(n,p) ≈ Poisson(np). When n is large and p not small, use Normal approximation instead (Central Limit Theorem).

edgeCases.q: What's the difference between Geometric definitions?
edgeCases.a: Two conventions: (1) trials until first success (support {1,2,…}), (2) failures before first success (support {0,1,…}). Both common; check which definition is used. Expectations differ by 1: E[X] = 1/p vs (1-p)/p.

sources.label: Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 3
Ross — Introduction to Probability Models (12th ed.)
